It isn't like _all_ compilation regressed, this is just a very specific and unfortunate case, it'll always happen that some compilation times go down, other go up, it's just how LLVM is.

Actually, I think it is a very general case.
Sorry for this long post ...

Today I stumbled across FilePathsBase, where I observe that:
```julia
using FilePathsBase

t1 = ("hello", "world")
t2 = ("hello", "world", "foo")

@time WindowsPath(t1)
#   0.019310 seconds (27.02 k allocations: 1.816 MiB, 99.81% compilation time)
# p"hello/world"

@time WindowsPath(t2)
#   0.019089 seconds (27.80 k allocations: 1.861 MiB, 99.82% compilation time)
# p"hello/world/foo"
```
Similarly, `PosixPath()` is recompiled for each different number of elements, although only the constructor is called.

One might argue, that it was not a wise decision to store segments in Tuples, but I'm quite sure that little people know about this detail.

But let's come have a closer look at `WindowsPath` and switch the storage type to a `Vector()` and even spend some `@nospecialise`s
```julia
struct WindowsPath2 <: FilePathsBase.SystemPath
    segments::Vector{String}
    root::String
    drive::String
    separator::String

    function WindowsPath2(
        @nospecialize(segments::Union{Vector, Tuple}), 
        root::String,
        drive::String,
        separator::String=FilePathsBase.WIN_PATH_SEPARATOR
    )

        return new(filter!(!isempty, collect(segments)), root, drive, separator)
    end
end

function WindowsPath2(@nospecialize segments::Union{Vector, Tuple}; root="", drive="", separator="\\")
    return WindowsPath2(segments, root, drive, separator)
end
```
We see that WindowsPath2 is still recompiled:
```julia
@time WindowsPath(t1)
#   0.039903 seconds (34.32 k allocations: 2.294 MiB, 99.82% compilation time)
# p"hello\world"

@time WindowsPath(t2)
#  0.012491 seconds (17.02 k allocations: 1.120 MiB, 99.68% compilation time)
# p"hello\world\foo"
```
The reason is that, as mentioned above, `collect()` is as well recompiled for each new type.
I find this quite unfortunate, as conversion between NTuples and Vectors should be really optimised in Julia.

With the following lines we can, however, remedy recompilation for `collect()`

```julia
function Base.collect(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(t::Tuple)) where T
    res = Vector{T}(undef, length(t))
    i = 1
    for x in t # in eachindex(t)
        res[i] = x #t[i]
        i += 1
    end
    res
end

function Base.collect(@nospecialize t::Tuple)
    if isempty(t)
        []
    elseif t isa NTuple
        T = typeof(t)
        Base.collect(T.parameters[1], t)
    else
        Base._collect(1:1 #= Array =#, t, Base.IteratorEltype(t), Base.IteratorSize(t))
    end
end
```
and then find:
```julia
@time WindowsPath(t1)
#   0.028509 seconds (11.37 k allocations: 754.023 KiB, 99.71% compilation time: 52% of which was recompilation)
# p"hello\world"

@time WindowsPath(t2)
#   0.000023 seconds (2 allocations: 128 bytes)
# p"hello\world\foo"
```
Maybe, I'm overlooking something here?
The remedy of a faster `collect()` of NTuples is certainly an improvement. But I think the same kind of improvement should be done internally for any broadcasting over NTuples.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1682596273/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This PR addresses a topic that came up in #49671.

Currently, dynamic conversion of NTuples to Vectors by `collect()` triggers a new compilation
This PR offers a julia solution to this problem.

There might be other solutions to this issue, most probably compiler-related, but that's certainly out of reach for me.

Before the patch `collect(t::Tuple)` is recompiled for each number of tuple elements
```julia
julia> n = 0;

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.110986 seconds (79.05 k allocations: 5.343 MiB, 99.61% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.012787 seconds (241 allocations: 11.375 KiB, 99.69% compilation time)
```
after the patch, compilation happens only once:
```julia    
julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.029778 seconds (18.11 k allocations: 1.207 MiB, 99.68% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.000037 seconds (15 allocations: 688 bytes)
```

of all, this is my first PR to julia, so please be patient with my if I should have missed something.

This PR addresses a topic that came up in #49671.

Currently, dynamic conversion of NTuples to Vectors by `collect()` triggers a new compilation
This PR offers a julia solution to this problem.

There might be other solutions to this issue, most probably compiler-related, but that's certainly out of reach for me.

Before the patch `collect(t::Tuple)` is recompiled for each number of tuple elements
```julia
julia> n = 0;

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.110986 seconds (79.05 k allocations: 5.343 MiB, 99.61% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.012787 seconds (241 allocations: 11.375 KiB, 99.69% compilation time)
```
after the patch, compilation happens only once:
```julia    
julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.029778 seconds (18.11 k allocations: 1.207 MiB, 99.68% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.000037 seconds (15 allocations: 688 bytes)
```

I digged a little deeper on performance (which I should have done before posting this...). But then I would have missed your comment and the following learning
- `t isa NTuple` is costly and increases with the tuple length
The performance drops significantly, up to factors of 10 and more. So I'm not sure whether switching the method for higher number of elements is that reasonable.
(Would it be possible to have the compiler track whether a Tuple is an NTuple...?)

Then I thought, we might offer only a specialisation of `collect(String, tuple)`, but this would again need ` isa NTuple`, as `collect(String, itr)` already exists.

So if ever, I would tend to offer a separate function `collect_ntuple(T, ntuple)`. However, this function is still a factor 4-5 slower than the specialised collect(T, itr). The developer would need to decide, whether a largely varying number of tuple elements is expected.

Finally I wondered, whether I could somehow find a faster solution and ended up in defining an `unsafe_collect_ntuple(T, ntuple)`. That one is indeed faster, so an experienced developer might benefit from such a function.

(I need to acknowledge the author of this post on stack overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54889057/how-to-get-a-ptr-to-an-element-of-an-ntuple/54898127#54898127)
```julia
function unsafe_ntuple_copyto!(dest::Array{T}, doffs, @nospecialize(src::Tuple), soffs, n) where T
    n == 0 && return dest
    t1 = Base.@_gc_preserve_begin dest
    t2 = Base.@_gc_preserve_begin src

    destp = pointer(dest, doffs)
    srcp = ccall(:jl_value_ptr, Ptr{Cvoid}, (Any,), src)
    soffs == 1 || (srcp += n * Base.elsize(T[]))
    if !Base.allocatedinline(T)
        ccall(:jl_array_ptr_copy, Cvoid, (Any, Ptr{Cvoid}, Any, Ptr{Cvoid}, Int),
              dest, destp, src, srcp, n)
    elseif isbitstype(T)
        ccall(:memmove, Ptr{Cvoid}, (Ptr{Cvoid}, Ptr{Cvoid}, Csize_t),
              destp, srcp, n * Base.aligned_sizeof(T))
    elseif isbitsunion(T)
        ccall(:memmove, Ptr{Cvoid}, (Ptr{Cvoid}, Ptr{Cvoid}, Csize_t),
              destp, srcp, n * Base.aligned_sizeof(T))
        # copy selector bytes
        ccall(:memmove, Ptr{Cvoid}, (Ptr{Cvoid}, Ptr{Cvoid}, Csize_t),
              ccall(:jl_array_typetagdata, Ptr{UInt8}, (Any,), dest) + doffs - 1,
              ccall(:jl_array_typetagdata, Ptr{UInt8}, (Any,), src) + soffs - 1,
              n)
    else
        _unsafe_copyto!(dest, doffs, src, soffs, n)
    end
    Base.@_gc_preserve_end t2
    Base.@_gc_preserve_end t1
    return dest
end

function collect_ntuple(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(t::Tuple)) where T
    res = Vector{T}(undef, length(t))
    i = 1
    for x in t
        @inbounds res[i] = x::T
        i += 1
    end
    res
end

function unsafe_collect_ntuple(::Type{T}, @nospecialize(t::Tuple)) where T
    return unsafe_ntuple_copyto!(Vector{T}(undef, length(t)), 1, t, 1, length(t))
end

function collect_tuple(@nospecialize t::Tuple)
    if isempty(t)
        []
    elseif t isa NTuple
        unsafe_collect_ntuple(typeof(t[1]), t)
    else
        Base._collect(1:1 #= Array =#, t, Base.HasEltype(), Base.HasLength())
    end
end
```


```julia
julia> n = 10000
julia> t1 = tuple(string.(1:n)...);
julia> t2 = tuple(:a, string.(1:n-1)...);

julia> @btime collect(t1);
  13.600 μs (2 allocations: 78.17 KiB)

julia> @btime collect_tuple(t1);
  235.600 μs (4 allocations: 78.20 KiB)

julia> @btime collect(String, t1);
  14.500 μs (2 allocations: 78.17 KiB)

julia> @btime collect_ntuple(String, t1);
  51.800 μs (2 allocations: 78.17 KiB)

julia> @btime unsafe_collect_ntuple(String, t1);
  7.733 μs (3 allocations: 78.19 KiB)

# test whether unsafe_collect_ntuple will be recompiled for a new number of elements
julia> n += 1; t1 = tuple(string.(1:n)...); @time unsafe_collect_ntuple(String, t1);
  0.000024 seconds (3 allocations: 78.250 KiB)
```
So my current proposal would be to define new functions `collect_ntuples()` and `unsafe_collect_ntuples()` and keep the latter unexported. (`collect_tuples()` was just defined here to show the bad performance of `t is NTuple` if the function is not specialised.)

Perhaps there are other ways to speed up `collect_ntuple()`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1683687663/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

of all, this is my first PR to julia, so please be patient with my if I should have missed something.

This PR addresses a topic that came up in #49671.

Currently, dynamic conversion of NTuples to Vectors by `collect()` triggers a new compilation
This PR offers a julia solution to this problem.

There might be other solutions to this issue, most probably compiler-related, but that's certainly out of reach for me.

Before the patch `collect(t::Tuple)` is recompiled for each number of tuple elements
```julia
julia> n = 0;

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.110986 seconds (79.05 k allocations: 5.343 MiB, 99.61% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.012787 seconds (241 allocations: 11.375 KiB, 99.69% compilation time)
```
after the patch, compilation happens only once:
```julia    
julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.029778 seconds (18.11 k allocations: 1.207 MiB, 99.68% compilation time)

julia> n += 1; @time collect(tuple(string.(1:n)...),);
  0.000037 seconds (15 allocations: 688 bytes)
```

And I was not hitting the compile time of 150ms but the compile time of 20-40s.

I read more about the differences of tuples and vectors (e.g. https://discourse.julialang.org/t/when-to-use-tuples/18675/18) to understand the underlying principles. Although it's getting a bit off-topic, I want to share my basic learning (also to have it verified by the more professional julia guys here ;-) )
- broadcasting over tuples (even NTuples) is only efficient for small tuples
- arrays should be preferred over tuples for lists of varying length
- if tuples cannot be avoided , compile time of comprehensions are shorter than for broadcasting syntax, e.g.

```julia
julia> t = Tuple(Symbol("x$(lpad(n, 5, '0'))") for n in 1:100);

julia> @time String.(t);
  0.230550 seconds (529.87 k allocations: 34.547 MiB, 16.87% gc time, 98.88% compilation time)

julia> @time tuple(String(f) for f in t)
  0.026668 seconds (2.04 k allocations: 139.259 KiB, 97.73% compilation time)
```

I wonder whether compiling of NTuples couldn't be better optimized so that the compiled methods are not specialised on N. Even collect(::NTuple{N, T}) is newly compiled for each N.
